IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles an important problem—parsing broad-coverage semantic structures that require multiple parents, nonterminal nodes and discontinuous units—and provides concrete methods (conversion protocols and a new transition-based BSP with remote-edge transitions) to address it. The empirical demonstration (nearly 70% labeled F-score on UCCA corpora), availability of code and detailed hyperparameters make the approach likely to be reused and built upon by researchers exploring graph-based semantic parsing. While the lossy nature of conversions and deferred handling of implicit units limit completeness, the work substantially advances tooling and evaluation for BSS and opens clear new directions for parsers that go beyond tree-structured outputs. | B(4): The paper tackles an important practical problem—broad-coverage semantic parsing for structures requiring multiple parents, nonterminal nodes and discontinuous units—and provides concrete technical contributions (conversion protocols between UCCA and dependency/discontinuous-constituency representations, and a novel transition-based parser BSP with new remote-edge transitions). The empirical results (nearly 70% labeled F-score on in- and out-of-domain UCCA corpora), availability of code, and the demonstrated compatibility of transition-based methods suggest the work will meaningfully influence researchers and tool-builders assessing and extending parsing technology for BSS. While it is not fully comprehensive (conversion is lossy and implicit units are deferred), the methods and resources open clear follow-on research directions and are likely to be widely adopted within semantic parsing research.

While the lossy nature of conversions and deferred handling of implicit units limit completeness, the work substantially advances tooling and evaluation for BSS and opens clear new directions for parsers that go beyond tree-structured outputs.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This is squarely within the scope of a top NLP/ML venue: it tackles broad-coverage semantic parsing (UCCA) and the structural properties required for BSS (multiple parents, nonterminal nodes, discontinuous units). The paper proposes both conversion protocols and a novel transition-based parser (BSP) with new transitions and features, trains with an averaged structured perceptron, and compares to strong baselines (MaltParser variants, Stack LSTM, UPARSE) on English UCCA corpora, reporting nearly 70% labeled F-score and providing code. The methodology, datasets, metrics, and thorough related work make it well positioned for the target audience and community. | B(5): This paper falls squarely within the scope of a top NLP/ML venue: it addresses broad-coverage semantic parsing (UCCA) and introduces a novel transition-based parser (BSP) that supports multiple parents, nonterminal nodes and discontinuous units. The methodology and evaluation meet community standards, including conversion protocols to related formalisms, comparisons to strong baselines (MaltParser, Stack LSTM, UPARSE), reported hyperparameters, and labeled F-score results on English UCCA corpora. The availability of code, thorough related-work coverage, and clear statement of limitations further position it appropriately for the venue's audience.

The paper proposes both conversion protocols and a novel transition-based parser (BSP) with new transitions and features, trains with an averaged structured perceptron, and compares to strong baselines (MaltParser variants, Stack LSTM, UPARSE) on English UCCA corpora, reporting nearly 70% labeled F-score and providing code. The methodology, datasets, metrics, and thorough related work make it well positioned for the target audience and community.

REPLICABILITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper reports hyperparameters and training details (averaged structured perceptron, MINUPDATE) and the authors provide code, and they name the English UCCA corpora used (Abend & Rappoport, 2013), so core model and data access appear available. Evaluation metric (labeled F-score) and conversion protocols are described, enabling comparable evaluation. However, the summary does not describe the data preprocessing pipeline or how implicit units and lossy conversions were handled in practice, which are implementation details that could affect exact replication. Overall, most essentials are present but a few practical preprocessing/representation details are missing. | B(4): The paper reports hyperparameters and training details (averaged structured perceptron, MINUPDATE) and the code is available, and it specifies the datasets used (English UCCA corpora), making core model replication feasible. However, the summary does not indicate a clearly described data preprocessing pipeline, and the authors note lossy conversions and deferred handling of implicit units, which are practical details that could affect exact reproduction. Overall, most necessary details are present but a few dataset/preprocessing specifics appear missing.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper reports hyperparameters and training details (averaged structured perceptron, MINUPDATE) and the authors provide code, and they name the English UCCA corpora used (Abend & Rappoport, 2013), so core model and data access appear available. Evaluation metric (labeled F-score) and conversion protocols are described, enabling comparable evaluation. However, the summary does not describe the data preprocessing pipeline or how implicit units and lossy conversions were handled in practice, which are implementation details that could affect exact replication. Overall, most essentials are present but a few practical preprocessing/representation details are missing.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper reports hyperparameters and training details (averaged structured perceptron, MINUPDATE) and the code is available, and it specifies the datasets used (English UCCA corpora), making core model replication feasible. However, the summary does not indicate a clearly described data preprocessing pipeline, and the authors note lossy conversions and deferred handling of implicit units, which are practical details that could affect exact reproduction. Overall, most necessary details are present but a few dataset/preprocessing specifics appear missing.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and supported by experimental evidence: it defines BSS properties, provides conversion protocols, introduces BSP with remote-edge transitions and specialized features, and reports nearly 70% labeled F-score on UCCA corpora. The experimental methodology is generally sound, using multiple competitive baselines (conversion to dependency and discontinuous-constituency with MaltParser, a Stack-LSTM arc-standard parser, and UPARSE) and reporting hyperparameters and code, though no statistical significance tests are mentioned and they could not compare to dependency DAG parsers due to unavailable code. The authors acknowledge key limitations (lossy conversions and deferred implicit-unit parsing), so conclusions about BSP’s effectiveness are justified while minor baseline and evaluation omissions remain. | B(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and supported by experimental evidence: conversion protocols, a new transition-based BSP parser, and complementary baselines (MaltParser variants, Stack-LSTM, UPARSE) are evaluated on in- and out-of-domain UCCA corpora, achieving near 70% labeled F-score. There are no substantive mathematical derivations or proofs required beyond standard structured-perceptron training (MINUPDATE) and transition-system definitions, which appear correct and complete for the described methods. The experimental methodology is generally sound with appropriate baselines and datasets, but it omits existing dependency DAG parsers (due to unavailable code) and no statistical significance testing is reported, which are minor shortcomings. The authors acknowledge key limitations (lossy conversions, implicit units deferred), and the conclusions are reasonable given the presented results.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and supported by experimental evidence: it defines BSS properties, provides conversion protocols, introduces BSP with remote-edge transitions and specialized features, and reports nearly 70% labeled F-score on UCCA corpora. The experimental methodology is generally sound, using multiple competitive baselines (conversion to dependency and discontinuous-constituency with MaltParser, a Stack-LSTM arc-standard parser, and UPARSE) and reporting hyperparameters and code, though no statistical significance tests are mentioned and they could not compare to dependency DAG parsers due to unavailable code. The authors acknowledge key limitations (lossy conversions and deferred implicit-unit parsing), so conclusions about BSP’s effectiveness are justified while minor baseline and evaluation omissions remain.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and supported by experimental evidence: conversion protocols, a new transition-based BSP parser, and complementary baselines (MaltParser variants, Stack-LSTM, UPARSE) are evaluated on in- and out-of-domain UCCA corpora, achieving near 70% labeled F-score. There are no substantive mathematical derivations or proofs required beyond standard structured-perceptron training (MINUPDATE) and transition-system definitions, which appear correct and complete for the described methods. The experimental methodology is generally sound with appropriate baselines and datasets, but it omits existing dependency DAG parsers (due to unavailable code) and no statistical significance testing is reported, which are minor shortcomings. The authors acknowledge key limitations (lossy conversions, implicit units deferred), and the conclusions are reasonable given the presented results.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper reports thorough empirical evaluation on in-domain and out-of-domain UCCA corpora, compares the proposed BSP to multiple baselines (conversion-based using MaltParser, Stack LSTM, UPARSE), reports labeled F-scores (best ≈70%), provides code and hyperparameters, and discusses conversion losses—supporting the main claims. However, the summary does not mention ablation studies or sensitivity analyses of the new transitions/features, and some relevant comparisons (existing dependency DAG parsers) were omitted due to unavailable code; handling of implicit units is deferred. These are minor but notable gaps, so the work is solid overall but not exhaustive. | B(4): The experimental evaluation is solid: the authors test both conversion-based strategies and their new BSP on in-domain and out-of-domain English UCCA corpora and compare to multiple baselines (MaltParser variants, Stack-LSTM parser, UPARSE), reporting labeled F-scores (~70% for best parser). They report code and hyperparameters, and provide conversion protocols, which supports reproducibility. However, the paper does not appear to include ablation studies or sensitivity analyses of the new transitions/features, and it omits evaluation against dependency DAG parsers only due to unavailable code—both minor gaps. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and limitations (lossy conversions, deferred implicit unit parsing) are explicitly discussed.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper reports thorough empirical evaluation on in-domain and out-of-domain UCCA corpora, compares the proposed BSP to multiple baselines (conversion-based using MaltParser, Stack LSTM, UPARSE), reports labeled F-scores (best ≈70%), provides code and hyperparameters, and discusses conversion losses—supporting the main claims. However, the summary does not mention ablation studies or sensitivity analyses of the new transitions/features, and some relevant comparisons (existing dependency DAG parsers) were omitted due to unavailable code; handling of implicit units is deferred. These are minor but notable gaps, so the work is solid overall but not exhaustive.

reviewer_b.justification: The experimental evaluation is solid: the authors test both conversion-based strategies and their new BSP on in-domain and out-of-domain English UCCA corpora and compare to multiple baselines (MaltParser variants, Stack-LSTM parser, UPARSE), reporting labeled F-scores (~70% for best parser). They report code and hyperparameters, and provide conversion protocols, which supports reproducibility. However, the paper does not appear to include ablation studies or sensitivity analyses of the new transitions/features, and it omits evaluation against dependency DAG parsers only due to unavailable code—both minor gaps. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and limitations (lossy conversions, deferred implicit unit parsing) are explicitly discussed.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates the proposed BSP against several reasonable baselines (conversion-based approaches using MaltParser, a Stack LSTM arc-standard parser, and UPARSE) on the same English UCCA corpora with labeled F-score, so comparisons appear fair and consistent. The authors explicitly analyze conversion loss and argue why a direct transition-based parser is needed, and related work coverage is described as thorough. They do acknowledge a notable omission—existing dependency DAG parsers were not included because code was unavailable—which is a minor but relevant gap. Overall the comparisons are good with this minor omission. | B(4): The paper compares its parser to appropriate existing methods (conversion-based approaches using MaltParser, a Stack-LSTM arc-standard parser (Dyer et al., 2015), and UPARSE) on the same English UCCA corpora with labeled F-score, so comparisons appear fair. The authors explicitly discuss the lossy nature of conversions and note they could not evaluate existing dependency DAG parsers because code was unavailable, showing awareness of limitations. Related work coverage is described as thorough, but the omission of dependency-DAG baselines (and any unavailable recent DAG implementations) is a notable, acknowledged gap—hence a near-complete but not fully comprehensive comparison.

The authors explicitly analyze conversion loss and argue why a direct transition-based parser is needed, and related work coverage is described as thorough. ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents notable novel elements: it formalizes structural properties required for Broad-coverage Semantic Structures (BSS) and develops conversion protocols between UCCA and dependency/discontinuous-constituency formalisms to evaluate existing parsers. Most importantly, it proposes a new transition-based parser (BSP) that directly supports multiple parents, nonterminal nodes and discontinuous units via novel transitions (e.g., LEFTREMOTEX/RIGHT-REMOTEX) and specialized features, which is a non-trivial extension of standard transition systems. The authors clearly differentiate these contributions from prior work (conversion baselines and existing parsers) and demonstrate their approach empirically, so the contribution is a clear, notable advance rather than purely incremental. | B(4): The paper makes a notable novel contribution by defining Broad-Coverage Semantic Structures (BSS) requirements and proposing a new transition-based parser (BSP) that directly supports multiple parents, nonterminal nodes and discontinuous units via novel transitions (LEFTREMOTEX/RIGHT-REMOTEX) and specialized features. The development of conversion protocols between UCCA and dependency/discontinuous-constituency formalisms is an additional original methodological contribution used to rigorously assess existing parsers. These techniques are non-trivial extensions of transition-based parsing and are clearly differentiated from prior work (conversion baselines and existing parsers), so the work would inform experts without being a radical paradigm shift.

The authors clearly differentiate these contributions from prior work (conversion baselines and existing parsers) and demonstrate their approach empirically, so the contribution is a clear, notable advance rather than purely incremental.

The development of conversion protocols between UCCA and dependency/discontinuous-constituency formalisms is an additional original methodological contribution used to rigorously assess existing parsers. CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well organized and follows a clear logical flow (defines BSS properties, presents conversion protocols, introduces the BSP transition-based parser, then reports experiments on UCCA corpora). The main contributions are stated early and repeatedly (need for BSS-capable parsers, conversion protocols, and the novel BSP). Notation and operational details seem consistently presented (explicit transition names, training procedure, and reported hyperparameters), and the writing quality is described as good with thorough related-work coverage. One minor caveat is that the summary does not report on the quality of figures/tables or whether some technical sections (e.g., new transitions) could be dense for readers, so I rate presentation as very good but not flawless. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized with a logical flow (intro → methods describing conversion protocols and the BSP parser → experiments on English UCCA corpora → conclusions), and the main contributions are stated early (defining BSS properties, conversion protocols, and the novel BSP). The summary reports good writing quality, thorough related-work coverage, and that implementation details (code and hyperparameters) are provided, all of which support clear presentation of methodology and results. Because the summary does not indicate any major clarity problems but also does not claim exceptional presentation features, I rate it a 4 rather than a 5.

main_claims: Define the structural properties required for Broad-coverage Semantic Structures (BSS) and argue the need for parsers supporting multiple parents, nonterminal nodes and discontinuous units.
Develop conversion protocols between UCCA structures and related formalisms (dependency trees and discontinuous constituency trees) to assess existing parsing technology for BSS.
Propose a novel transition-based parser, Broad-coverage Semantic Parser (BSP), that directly supports multiple parents, nonterminal nodes and discontinuous units via new transitions and features.

Demonstrate experimentally on English UCCA-annotated corpora (in-domain and out-of-domain) that transition-based methods are effective for BSS parsing, achieving nearly 70% labeled F-score for the best parser.
_summary.methodology: Two complementary transition-based strategies: (1) convert UCCA graphs to dependency and discontinuous constituency representations and apply existing parsers; (2) build a new transition-based parser (BSP) that extends standard transitions (e.g., SHIFT, REDUCE, NODE, SWAP) with remote-edge transitions (LEFTREMOTEX, RIGHT-REMOTEX) and specialized features, trained with an averaged structured perceptron and MINUPDATE procedure.
baselines: Conversion-based methods (to dependency trees and discontinuous constituency) using MaltParser (arc-standard and arc-eager)
Stack LSTM-based arc-standard parser (Dyer et al., 2015)
limitations: Conversion to dependency or discontinuous-constituency formalisms is lossy because these formalisms are more restrictive than BSS.
UCCA graphs may contain implicit units with no correspondent in the text; parsing of these implicit units is deferred to future work.
The authors did not use existing dependency DAG parsers because their code could not be obtained.
